seed: trust vs suspicion; signal_vs_noise; split_vertical; diagonal_fall; stairs against ash_field; pressure_border; soft_glow_vs_hard_shadow; line; resilience_fragility | abstract variation request 160; target batch count 500
title: Fractured Symmetry
reasoning: This artwork embodies the emotional dichotomy of trust and suspicion through contrasting color fields. The color palette, influenced by 'love_betrayal', features deep reds and soft pinks to represent trust intertwined with dark maroon and gray, symbolizing suspicion. The left side leans heavily towards warmth, suggesting a secure base, while the right side introduces colder hues representing uncertainty, accentuated by a few off-kilter pixels that mimic the cracks of suspicion. The composition’s vertical split evokes an interplay of pressure and release, while the patterns of static and crack reflect both a foundation and disruption in relationships. The main collision occurs at the center of the piece, where the warm and cool hues intersect, creating tension and a dynamic flow outward, inviting contemplation of balance and resilience.

layout.name: split_vertical
layout.description: Left 4 columns are emotion A, right 4 columns are emotion B.

composition.name: symmetry_vs_break
composition.idea: A nearly mirrored image has one wrong or corrupted side.

referenceExample.title: Static Around a Bridge
referenceExample.pattern: cold broken islands interrupted by a warm connecting channel

generationRules: Pick 1 pattern type for emotion A.
Pick 1 pattern type for emotion B.
Pick 1 shape seed as the central object.
Apply 1 to 6 broken pixels to create imperfection.
Leave at least 10 percent negative space unless the mood is panic, rage, urgency, euphoria, or pressure.
Use the shape seed as an abstract organizing metaphor, not as a literal icon.
Make the two selected pattern types visibly collide, overlap, interrupt, or trade space.
Prefer 4 to 8 distinct hex colors, with at least one quiet field color and one accent color.
Keep the 8x8 grid readable at thumbnail size by preserving clear large-scale structure.
Let one emotion dominate 55 to 70 percent of the square unless the selected layout demands balance.
Use broken pixels as intentional disruptions, scars, repairs, leaks, or false echoes.
Avoid perfectly even distribution unless the chosen composition is control, serenity, clarity, or certainty.
Give the main collision a location: center, edge, corner, diagonal, border, or channel.
generationSpec.negativeSpaceRule: Leave at least 10 percent negative space unless the chosen emotional tone needs dense pressure.
